baseline.commit_subject: Add Mira Core Phase 20 server API validation
baseline.baseline_boundary: Phase 20 defines planned API/control-plane/status shapes only. Phase 21 may define pure request-in/response-out handler semantics, but no real server, listener, routes, HTTP/network path, database/store/file write, migration, queue, auth/session/key change, keygen, encryption/decryption, KMS/env-secret read, local execution, send, deploy, trade, memory/profile commit, export, deletion, restore, raw-content storage, or output-file behavior exists at this baseline.

purpose: Convert Phase 20 planned API/status shapes into a fixture-only contract for pure server handler/dispatcher semantics before any real server runtime exists. Phase 21 validates endpoint id plus redacted metadata envelope in, validation/status envelope out, with pure dispatch table behavior and no side effects.
nonGoals: No HTTP server, HTTP client, or network request.
No database, source-store, or file write.
No auth, token, credential, secret, or session change.
No environment secret read or export.
No browser, window, screenshot, or OCR access.
No external sends or customer sends.

scope: Build validation-only server handler/dispatcher assessment records against this fixture, if Builder is later delegated.

Handler semantics are pure request-in/response-out functions over endpoint id plus metadata envelope. They do not bind listeners, register routes, make network calls, write stores/files, enqueue jobs, mutate auth/session/key state, generate keys, encrypt/decrypt, read env secrets, execute locally, send, deploy, trade, commit memory/profile state, export, delete, restore, or store raw content.
Represent commit ce9e55d as the Phase 20 baseline and map the green validation-only dependency chain for Phases 14-20.

Emit handler registry shape, request envelope shape, response envelope shape, endpoint dispatch table, replay/idempotency/watermark/tombstone rules, binding requirements, status-only storage/delete/export/retention policies, local-arms/offline truth, target/risk gates, privacy/security boundaries, migration gates, evidence refs, and side-effect truth.
Accept only validation/status results for read-only status and safe upload/intent/deletion-export proposals; risky actions are blocked or review-required as specified.

Treat handler validity as validation-only. It never authorizes DB writes, storage writes, local execution, shell/PTY, Builder/Oracle direct server targeting, Tier 2+ work without review/block, customer sends, deploys, trades, memory/profile commits, model-processing proof, bridge green, raw restore, or resurrection of deleted local state.
hardRejects: Any server process, listener, route registration, HTTP client/server call, network request, database/store/file write, migration execution, queue write, auth/secret/token/session/key change, key generation, encryption, decryption, KMS call, env secret read, local execution, shell/PTY, browser/window access, send, customer send, deploy, trade, financial action, durable migration, memory/profile commit, export-file write, deletion, restore, raw-content storage, or output-file write occurs.

Any handler input/output contains raw comms, raw terminal, screenshot/OCR text, browser state, customer private content, secret material, side-profile content, profile-mismatch payloads, bearer tokens, cookies, session secrets, private keys, data keys, plaintext, raw ciphertext, raw payloads, decrypted content, or raw database payloads.

A handler registry or dispatch table implies a live HTTP route, listener, websocket, API call, database write, queue write, job, worker, delete/export action, deploy, send, trade, local execution, or output file.

A request envelope accepts missing endpoint id, missing scope, missing auth binding ref, missing signature ref, missing key reference ref, missing source watermark, missing idempotency/replay key, raw payload, expired request, replay, tombstone conflict, profile mismatch, side-profile payload, or high-risk action as allowed.
A response envelope marks socket connection as bridge green, delivery acceptance as model-processing proof, or handler dispatch as recipient model-processing proof.

Server handler validity is treated as authorization for DB writes, storage writes, local execution, shell/PTY, direct Builder/Oracle targeting, Tier 2+ action without review/block, customer sends, deploys, trades, memory/profile commits, raw restore, or resurrection of deleted state.

Phase 21 migration language implies a real handler runtime can exist before transport/TLS, auth middleware, replay store, rate limits, audit logging, schema migrations, encryption/KMS integration, deletion/export jobs, operator UI, monitoring/alerts, disaster recovery, profile isolation tests, red-team leakage tests, and route/handler integration tests are reviewed.

staticValidationRules.must: server_handler_assessment.baseline_commit and validation_report.baseline_commit are exactly ce9e55d, the Phase 20 server API validation baseline.

Phase 21 emits validation records only; it does not start a server, bind listeners, register routes, perform HTTP/network work, write stores/files, run migrations, create queues, mutate auth/session/key state, generate keys, encrypt, decrypt, call KMS, read env secrets, execute locally, send, deploy, trade, commit memory/profile data, export, delete, restore, store raw content, or write output files.
Dependency map includes Phases 14-20 with fixture paths, commits, green status, and validation-only boundaries.
Handler registry entries are pure functions with endpoint ids, input/output schemas, allowed decisions, blocked actions, no route registration, no listener, and no raw payload.

Request envelopes require endpoint id, request id, idempotency/replay keys, tenant/profile/device/session scope, auth/signature/key refs, source watermark, redacted metadata summary, risk tier, action class, target role, expiry, evidence refs, and no raw payload.
Response envelopes contain status/proposal records only with decision, reasons, safe next action, redaction summary, refs, no raw payload, no store, no execution, no network, and no output file.
Endpoint dispatch maps endpoint ids to handler ids without creating routes, listeners, HTTP calls, workers, queues, or side effects.

Same request produces same response; payload/scope changes alter idempotency; replay is rejected; watermark regression is rejected; stale snapshots are warned or rejected; tombstone wins; expired requests are blocked.

Handlers require auth_binding_ref, signature_ref, key_reference_ref, tenant/profile/device/session scope, role proof refs, target proof refs, and fail closed on mismatch without mutating auth/session/key state.
Storage, deletion, export, and retention handlers return status/proposal records only and never write stores, output files, delete records, export files, mutate retention, or restore raw state.
Local-arms status may report offline or unknown but cannot claim local execution, PTY, shell, browser/window access, bridge green, or model-processing proof.

Server-originated handler responses target Architect only; direct Builder/Oracle targets are blocked; Tier 0 is validation-only, Tier 1 requires local acceptance, Tier 2 is review-required or blocked, and Tier 3/Tier 4 are blocked.

Handler inputs and outputs contain no raw comms, terminal, OCR, browser, customer private, secret, side-profile, profile mismatch, bearer token, cookie, session secret, private key, data key, plaintext, raw ciphertext, raw payload, or decrypted content.

Socket connection is not bridge green; delivery acceptance is not model processing; handler dispatch is not model processing; proof requires registered Architect role discovery, Architect target proof, and recipient quote-back or equivalent model-response evidence.

Real handler runtime remains blocked until transport/TLS, auth middleware, replay store, rate limits, audit logging, schema migrations, encryption/KMS integration, deletion/export jobs, operator UI, monitoring/alerts, disaster recovery, profile isolation tests, red-team leakage tests, and route/handler integration tests exist.
All required Phase 21 acceptance gate IDs and blocker IDs are present and unique.

Assessment and validation output contain no secret-like strings, raw private content, raw payloads, server/listener/network/write claims, bridge/proof overclaims, direct Builder/Oracle target authorization, high-risk authorization claims, or live side-effect claims.

summaryAcceptanceCriteria: Fixture parses as JSON.
Baseline commit is pinned to ce9e55d.
Phase 21 maps Phases 14-20 as green validation-only dependencies and does not claim any dependency is a real server/API/handler runtime.

Phase 21 is fixture/spec-only and performs no runtime, server, listener, route, HTTP/network, DB/store/file write, migration, queue, auth/session mutation, key generation, encryption, decryption, KMS, env secret read, local execution, send, deploy, trade, memory/profile commit, export, deletion, restore, raw-content storage, or output-file side effect.
Server handler output shape includes server_handler_assessment and validation_report.
Handler registry contains pure functions only, with endpoint ids, input/output schemas, allowed decisions, blocked actions, no listener, no route registration, and no raw payload.

Request envelope requires endpoint_id, request_id, idempotency key, replay key, tenant/profile/device/session scope, auth binding ref, signature ref, key reference ref, source watermark, redacted metadata summary, payload hash/ref, risk tier, action class, requested_by, target_role, created_at, expires_at, evidence refs, and no raw payload.

Response envelope returns validation/status/proposal records only with decision, reasons, safe_next_action, redaction summary, response refs, evidence refs, review_required, expires_at, no raw payload, no store, no execution, no network, and no output file.
Dispatch table maps endpoint ids to handlers without creating routes, listeners, HTTP calls, workers, queues, or side effects.
Idempotency/replay/watermark/tombstone rules reject replay, reject watermark regression, block expired requests, make tombstone win, and prevent duplicate side effects.
Auth/signature/key/profile/device/session binding requirements are by reference only and fail closed on missing refs or scope mismatch.
Upload receive handler returns validation-only storage status; storage eligibility stays core_sync_safe or already-redacted core_sync_redacted and no store write occurs.
Deletion/export/retention handlers return status/proposal records only, require James-visible review, and never delete, export, mutate retention, restore, or write output files.
Local-arms/offline status stays honest and cannot imply local execution, PTY, shell, browser access, bridge green, or model-processing proof.
Server-originated handler target remains Architect-only; direct Builder/Oracle targets are blocked.
Tier 0 requests may be accepted for validation only, Tier 1 may become pending local acceptance, Tier 2 is review-required or blocked, and Tier 3/Tier 4 are blocked.

Handler inputs and outputs block raw comms, terminal, OCR, browser, customer private, secret, side-profile, profile mismatch, bearer token, cookie, session secret, private key, data key, plaintext, raw ciphertext, raw payload, and decrypted content.
Proof and bridge boundaries are preserved: socket alone is not bridge green, delivery acceptance is not model processing, and handler dispatch is not model processing.

Real handler runtime can become buildable later only after transport/TLS, auth middleware, replay store, rate limits, audit logging, schema migrations, encryption/KMS integration, deletion/export jobs, operator UI, monitoring/alerts, disaster recovery, profile isolation tests, red-team leakage tests, and route/handler integration tests exist.
Acceptance gate IDs and blocker IDs are complete and unique.

Forbidden output substrings cover secrets, bearer tokens, cookies, key material, plaintext/ciphertext/decrypted content, raw private content, server/listener/network/write claims, handler execution overclaims, bridge/proof overclaims, direct Builder/Oracle target authorization, high-risk authorization overclaims, and live side-effect claims.
